title: CLIP: Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining
concept: CLIP connects vision and language by training image and text encoders to match corresponding pairs in a shared space.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the output of a Siamese network's encoder branch?

options.text: An embedding vector representing the input image.
A probability distribution that sums to 1.

exercises.explain: Correct! Siamese encoders project input images into high-dimensional vector embeddings.

cards.title: Connecting words and pictures
cards.body: If you look at a photo of a sunset and read the phrase 'a beautiful sunset over the hills', your brain immediately connects the visual colors to the words. CLIP is an AI model built to create this exact connection between language and vision.

cards.title: What is CLIP?
cards.body: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) is a model developed by OpenAI that trains an Image Encoder and a Text Encoder together. The model maximizes the cosine similarity between matching image-text pairs in a shared embedding space, while minimizing similarity for mismatched pairs.

cards.title: A giant matching game
cards.body: Imagine a table covered with photos on one side and caption cards on the other. Your goal is to draw lines connecting each photo to its correct caption. CLIP plays this game with millions of web pairs, learning what concepts look like in language.

cards.title: Zero-shot classification
cards.body: To classify a photo as dog vs. cat using CLIP: you encode the photo. You also encode text prompts: 'a photo of a dog' and 'a photo of a cat'. You calculate which text vector is closest to the image vector. The closest one wins!

cards.title: Not trained on class indexes
cards.body: You might think CLIP was trained on standard labeled datasets like ImageNet. In reality, it was trained on 400 million raw image-caption pairs scraped from the internet, allowing it to learn general visual concepts without specific label constraints.

cards.title: CLIP Shared Space
cards.body: How image and text embeddings align in a shared space:

left.text:
Input: Photo of a red sports car
Output: Visual Embedding Vector
Aligned near car language concepts

right.text:
Input: 'a photo of a red sports car'
Output: Text Embedding Vector
Close distance in shared space

exercises.prompt: How does CLIP achieve zero-shot classification?
exercises.explain: CLIP matches the image vector against text prompt vectors in its pre-trained shared space; the closest matching text description represents the predicted class.

options.text: By comparing the image embedding with the embeddings of candidate text descriptions in a shared space
By retraining its weights on the test set before every prediction
By converting all input images into text files
By predicting coordinates of the text on the screen

exercises.prompt: True or false: CLIP consists of a single convolutional network that processes both images and text sentences simultaneously.
exercises.explain: False. CLIP uses two distinct encoders: an Image Encoder (like a ResNet or ViT) and a Text Encoder (a Transformer), mapping their outputs to a shared space.
Contrastive learning focuses on contrasting matching pairs (pushing them close) with mismatched pairs (pushing them apart).
exercises.sentence: CLIP is trained using a ___ loss to match correct image-text pairs and reject incorrect ones.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a matched pair that CLIP aims to bring close together in embedding space?
exercises.explain: Bringing matching semantic content from images and text close together is the core objective of CLIP contrastive training.

options.text: Image: A photo of a black cat | Text: 'a photo of a black cat'
Image: A photo of a black cat | Text: 'a rendering of a red truck'
Image: A blank white canvas | Text: 'a busy city street'

exercises.prompt: You want to deploy CLIP to classify satellite photos of deforestation. You have no labeled images. What is the correct implementation strategy?

options.text: Encode the satellite images. Encode prompts: 'satellite photo of forest' and 'satellite photo of deforestation'. Compute cosine similarity and pick the highest.
Manually label 5,000 photos, train a custom Softmax head, and replace the text encoder.
Convert the satellite images to grayscale, and train a regression model to estimate height.
CLIP cannot be used for this task because it only understands internet memes.

exercises.explain: Exactly! This is a zero-shot classification workflow. By leveraging CLIP's massive pre-trained knowledge of what 'forest' and 'deforestation' look like from web data, you can classify new images instantly without a single training label. Next, let's explore how we write these prompts!
